The format depends on what the GoSwitch/NOS API returns, and   [0m

[0m[48;2;30;46;30m [0m[3m[48;2;30;46;30mIn the CITr2 environment, an EXOS stack device (serial `2328F-20117`, chassisId `02:04:96:BA:67:20`) appeared as two separate tiles in the NVO UI. Clicking the duplicate tile returned HTTP 400. The root cause is two  [0m

[0m[48;2;30;46;30m `inferred[0m[3m[48;2;30;46;30m_device` row and creation of a new duplicate; and (2) `StartDeviceUndiscovery` uses `FindFirst` (LIMIT 1, ORDER BY id ASC) to locate the device before hard-deletion, silently skipping any additional          [0m
[0m[48;2;30;46;30m [0m[3m[48;2;30;46;30mduplicate rows with a higher UUID. A third latent bug — the `len > 1` case in `savePartiallyDiscoveredInferredDevice` being unhandled — compounds state after duplicates exist. The fix requires normalizing             [0m
[0m[48;2;30;46;30m [0m[3m[48;2;30;46;30m`RemoteChassisId` at LLDP ingestion in the physical-link correlator and changing `FindFirst` to `FindAll` in undiscovery, plus a `len > 1` dedup guard in the sparse-save path.[0m[38;2;108;112;134m[48;2;30;46;30m                                          [0m

 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mNow I have a much clearer picture of ALL the code sites. Let me compile the comprehensive list.[0m                                                                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[1m[38;2;148;226;213mdevice_correlator.go[0m[1m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m is a KEY additional site:[0m                                                                                                                                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mLines 199-205, 233 — Stack device full collection path (when a managed device gets fully discovered via collection, not just sparse save):[0m                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;49;50;68m```go[0m                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
   macAddress := devDB.BaseMacAddress                                                                                                                                                                                     
   [0m[38;2;203;166;247mif[0m devDB.IsStack {                                                                                                                                                                                                     
       macAddress = strings.ToLower(devDB.ChassisId)  [0m[38;2;108;112;134m// lowercase but NO colons![0m                                                                                                                                         
   }                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
   database.Filter([0m[38;2;166;227;161m"serial_number = ? OR mac_address = ?"[0m, devDB.SerialNumber, macAddress)                                                                                                                                
 [0m[38;2;49;50;68m```[0m                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mThis is the device correlator's full discovery path during complete collection of a managed stack device. It computes the MAC address by lowercasing the chassis ID without colons, then queries the database using      [0m
 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134meither the serial number or this formatted MAC address to find matching records.[0m                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;49;50;68m```[0m                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
   Storing the lowercase no-colon MAC in `infDevDB.MacAddress` represents a third source of inconsistent data formatting, alongside the physical link correlator and earlier partial discovery methods. The raw chassis

 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mNow I have a complete picture. Let me trace the full uptime pipeline for wireless APs and understand why NVO-18043 is a band-aid.[0m                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[1m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mThe uptime data flow for wireless APs:[0m                                                                                                                                                                                   
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;203;166;247m1. [0m[1m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device.SysUpTime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m is populated via [0m[38;2;148;226;213mGetDeviceDetails()[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m → GoSwitch → NOS API [0m[38;2;148;226;213m/state/system[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m → [0m[38;2;148;226;213mswitchState.SysUpTimeUint64[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m or [0m[38;2;148;226;213mswitchState.SysUpTime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m. This GoSwitch call happens during [0m[38;2;148;226;213mSystemCollector.FetchAll()[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m.[0m   
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;203;166;247m2. [0m[1m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mWhen does SystemCollector run for wireless APs?[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m It runs during [0m[38;2;148;226;213mStartDeviceDiscovery()[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m. For wireless APs, discovery is triggered by:[0m                                                                                   
     [0m[38;2;203;166;247m- [0m[38;2;148;226;213mAPDeviceDataCollection[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m Kafka events[0m                                                                                                                                                                                
     [0m[38;2;203;166;247m- [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mAn 8-hour periodic timer[0m                                                                                                                                                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;203;166;247m3. [0m[1m[38;2;148;226;213minferred_device.uptime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m gets set in two places: the Correlator copies [0m[38;2;148;226;213mSysUpTime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m over during initial onboarding, and NVO-18043 patches it in discovery by syncing the asset device uptime back to the inferred device.[0m  
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mThe Visualization view pulls from [0m[38;2;148;226;213minferred_device.uptime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m while the Network Devices view reads [0m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device.sys_up_time[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m — they're looking at different sources. The NVO-18043 fix is really just a workaround because     [0m
 [0m[38;2;148;226;213minferred_device.uptime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m only updates at first onboard or on connection events, whereas [0m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device.SysUpTime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m only refreshes when the SystemCollector runs during GoSwitch discovery, which is triggered by CAPWAP        [0m
 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mconnects or that 8-hour timer.[0m                                                                                                                                                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mFor wireless APs specifically, the real issue is that [0m[38;2;148;226;213minferred_device.uptime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m was never updated after initial onboarding, and [0m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device.SysUpTime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m itself is stale between discovery cycles — there's no real-time push [0m
 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mmechanism, so NVO has to poll GoSwitch for fresh values.  ([0m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device.sys_up_time[0m), eliminating the discrepancy between the Visualization and Network Devices views. [0m[1mBut both views are still showing a stale, frozen value.[0m                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[1m[38;2;250;179;135m### The True Root Cause — Three Compounding Gaps[0m                                                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[1m[38;2;250;179;135m#### Gap 1: [0m[38;2;148;226;213mXIQAPDeviceDataCollectionMessage.ConnectionUptime[0m[1m[38;2;250;179;135m is parsed but never used[0m                                                                                                                                   
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 The [0m[38;2;148;226;213mxiq-device-data-collection[0m Kafka message carries a [0m[38;2;148;226;213mconnection_uptime[0m field (mapped to [0m[38;2;148;226;213mmsg.ConnectionUptime[0m). It is populated in the RunOnce gRPC onboarding path ([0m[38;2;148;226;213mgrpc_onboarding_wireless.go:321[0m) but is [0m[1mcompletely [0m
 [0m[1mdropped[0m in [0m[38;2;148;226;213mCreateDeviceConnectionDetailAndLocationModelsFromDataCollection()[0m — it is not written to [0m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device_connection_detail[0m or anywhere else. NVO receives this value from XIQ on every data-collection event and 
 immediately discards it.                                                                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;49;50;68m│ [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m⚠️ Note: [0m[3m[38;2;148;226;213mconnection_uptime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m from XIQ is the [0m[1m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mCAPWAP session uptime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m (time since last XIQ connect), not device [0m[3m[38;2;148;226;213mSysUpTime[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m since last reboot. It cannot be directly used for the "device uptime" displayed in EP1. But its   [0m
 [0m[38;2;49;50;68m│ [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mpresence shows XIQ is providing some uptime data — just the wrong kind.[0m                                                                                                                                                
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[1m[38;2;250;179;135m#### Gap 2: [0m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device.SysUpTime[0m[1m[38;2;250;179;135m is only refreshed via GoSwitch polling — every 8 hours for wireless APs[0m                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;148;226;213mSysUpTime[0m is populated in [0m[38;2;148;226;213mGetDeviceDetails()[0m via a GoSwitch NOS-API call. For wireless APs, this only runs during [0m[38;2;148;226;213mStartDeviceDiscovery()[0m, which is triggered by:                                                         
 [0m[38;2;203;166;247m- [0m[38;2;148;226;213mxiq-device-data-collection[0m Kafka events (CAPWAP connect triggers — not periodic on their own)                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;203;166;247m- [0mThe 8-hour [0m[38;2;148;226;213mPeriodicDiscoveryForWirelessInMinutes[0m scheduler                                                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 So between discovery runs, [0m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device.SysUpTime[0m is a [0m[1mfrozen snapshot[0m timestamped at the last GoSwitch poll. It monotonically increases on the device hardware but NVO doesn't track elapsed time.                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[1m[38;2;250;179;135m#### Gap 3: No elapsed-time correction at read time[0m                                                                                                                                                                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 When EP1 requests device uptime (via the NVO REST API / view SQL), NVO returns the raw [0m[38;2;148;226;213minferred_device.uptime[0m column value — which is the GoSwitch snapshot value frozen at poll time. There is no correction for        
 elapsed time since the snapshot was taken (i.e., no [0m[38;2;148;226;213msnapshot + (now - updated_at)[0m computation).                                                                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 For an AP that was last polled 6 hours ago showing [0m[38;2;148;226;213mSysUpTime = 10 days[0m, EP1 shows [0m[38;2;148;226;213m10 days[0m instead of the correct [0m[38;2;148;226;213m10 days 6 hours[0m.                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[1m[38;2;250;179;135m### True Fix[0m                                                                                                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 The correct fix is to [0m[1mcompute live uptime dynamically at read time[0m:                                                                                                                                                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 [0m[38;2;49;50;68m```[0m                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
   displayed_uptime = asset_device.sys_up_time + EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (NOW() - asset_device.updated_at)) * 100                                                                                                              
 [0m[38;2;49;50;68m```[0m                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 (centiseconds correction, using GORM's [0m[38;2;148;226;213mupdated_at[0m as the GoSwitch poll timestamp)                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 This can be implemented at the PCM view SQL layer or in the NVO API response builder — no GoSwitch polling frequency change required, no XIQ changes, no schema changes. The displayed uptime stays live between polls.  
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 A secondary fix: ensure [0m[38;2;148;226;213minferred_device.uptime[0m is also kept in sync during the periodic 8-hour GoSwitch discovery run, not only on CONNECT/DISCONNECT events (currently [0m[38;2;148;226;213msystem_collector.go[0m updates                      
 [0m[38;2;148;226;213masset_device.SysUpTime[0m via [0m[38;2;148;226;213mPersistAssetDeviceWithAssociations[0m but never back-propagates to [0m[38;2;148;226;213minferred_device.uptime[0m).

 [0m[38;2;49;50;68m```[0m

This is the critical piece: the NOS-API/gRPC [0m[38;2;148;226;213mDeviceDetail.system_up_time[0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134m  [0m
 [0m[3m[38;2;108;112;134mfor APs is already a boot epoch timestamp in ms, and GoSwitch correctly converts it by subtracting from the current time to get the actual uptime, then dividing by 10 to get centiseconds. But then NVO stores this     [0m
